articles.title: Should You Leave a Ceiling Fan Running All Day? We Settle the Debate Once and for All
articles.content:
有些家庭习惯会变成第二天性。你离开房间时会关灯，会锁前门。而在盛夏时节，你可能会让吊扇一直开着，以为这样能让房间保持凉爽，直到你回来。这是一个很容易做出的假设——也是一个引发许多争论的观点。

但是一整天都开着吊扇真的有用吗？我们咨询了Thumbtack专业顾问委员会成员、马里兰州暖通空调（HVAC）公司Climate Control Systems的老板马龙·钱伯斯。他将为我们澄清这个备受关注的话题。

夏季高温正在加剧，请继续阅读以了解真相。

如果您不在房间里，答案是否定的。尽管你可能认为吊扇确实能冷却空气，钱伯斯指出：“它实际上是在你的身体上产生冷却效果。”

原因如下：当空气流过皮肤时，它会加速汗液的蒸发。钱伯斯将其比作吸湿排汗运动服帮助将水分从身体带走的方式。结果是：你感觉更凉爽，尽管房间本身的温度并没有改变。

空调的工作方式不同。它不是冷却你的身体，而是从家中去除热量和湿气。吊扇可以帮助在你待在房间时循环经过调节的空气，但仅凭它本身，不会将80度的房间变成75度。正如钱伯斯所说：“你的风扇实际上并没有在给房子降温。你可能因为风扇产生的空气流动而感到凉爽，但房间里的温度就是温度。”

如果没有人享受微风，让风扇一直运行会有几个缺点。

吊扇效率很高，但运行它们并非没有成本。大多数现代吊扇，包括能源之星型号，根据尺寸和速度，大约消耗15到100瓦的电能。这听起来比空调要小，但日积月累就相当可观。

如果一台中等价位的风扇连续运行一个月，可能消耗50到100多千瓦时的电力。在许多美国家庭中，这足以每月每台风扇增加大约10到20美元的电费。整个夏天都运行它，你很容易就要花掉几顿外卖晚餐的钱来为一个没人的房间降温。

这听起来有违直觉，但每个电动马达在工作时都会产生热量作为副产品。吊扇不会把你的客厅变成桑拿房，但它确实会轻微抵消你试图保持的冷却效果。

在封闭的房间里，那小小的热量输出加上持续的空气流动，意味着风扇并没有主动降低温度。相反，它只是在重新分配已经存在的空气。钱伯斯说：“风扇不会产生所谓的冷空气……只有热量被移动，或者被空调移除。”

因此，虽然你不会在温度计上感觉到差异，但风扇正在“预冷”空房间的想法是站不住脚的。

像任何带有电机的电器一样，吊扇的寿命是有限的，以运行小时数来衡量。大多数家用吊扇在正常使用下的设计寿命约为10到15年，但持续运行会加速这一进程。

每一个不必要的运行小时都在累积：轴承承受更多压力，随时间产生更多振动，更有可能需要比预期更早进行维护或更换。换句话说，不是说风扇明天就会坏，而是你在浪费它的使用寿命，却没有获得任何真正的好处。

如果叶片不定期清洁，会积累颗粒，每次风扇旋转时都会被重新分布。这不仅仅是一个美观问题。室内空气中的灰尘是一种已知刺激物，对于有过敏或哮喘的人来说，可能会使症状恶化。

钱伯斯强调维护很重要。他说：“你肯定想保持清洁。风扇叶片越脏，运行所需的电力就越多。”换句话说，灰尘被吹散，使电机工作更辛苦，效率降低。

钱伯斯建议大约每月清洁一次吊扇叶片，或者在有宠物或灰尘水平较高的家庭中更频繁清洁。这是一项小家务，但对保持空气质量和效率都很重要。

吊扇仍然是保持舒适的最好工具之一。你只需要正确使用它。

在夏天，叶片应该逆时针旋转，将空气向下推送。大多数吊扇在电机外壳附近有一个小开关，让你可以随着季节改变叶片方向。在冬天，将风扇切换到低速顺时针旋转，有助于回收聚集在天花板附近的暖空气。

为了帮助空调更高效地工作，钱伯斯还建议在热量进入室内之前就阻挡它。遮光窗帘或百叶窗可以在一天中最热的时候减少太阳热量增加，紫外线阻挡窗膜（大多数家居用品店都有售）可以帮助阳光充足的房间不会那么快升温。

合适的风扇可以产生比许多房主意识到的更大的影响。钱伯斯说，人们经常根据外观选择更小、更装饰性的风扇，但太小而无法有效循环空气的风扇，对于较大的空间需要更长的叶片，能够在整个房间内移动空气，而不仅仅是在固定装置正下方。

如果你想在出门时让风扇继续运行，你并不孤单。钱伯斯说：“我们经常看到人们只是让风扇一直运行。”但他们所依赖的冷却效果并没有发生。把微风留给你真正在房间里的时候，让空调做它被设计来做的工作。
articles.summary:
Some home habits become second nature. You turn off the lights when you leave a room. You lock the front door. And in the middle of summer, you might leave the ceiling fan running, convinced it’ll keep the room cool until you get back. It’s an easy assumption to make—and one that has fueled plenty of debate.

But does leaving a fan on all day actually work? We consulted Marlon Chambers, a Thumbtack pro advisory board member and the owner of Climate Control Systems, a Maryland-based heating, ventilation and air conditioning (HVAC) company. He’s going to set us straight on this very timely debate and strategy.

Summer heat is cranking up, so read on to learn the truth.

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more home, cleaning, humor, travel, tech and fun facts all week long.

Should you leave a ceiling fan on all day?

If you’re not going to be in the room, the answer is no. Despite what you might think, a ceiling fan doesn’t really cool the air. “It’s actually creating the effect of cooling on your body,” Chambers says.

Here’s why: As air moves across your skin, it speeds up the evaporation of sweat. Chambers likens it to the way moisture-wicking athletic clothing helps pull moisture away from your body. The result? You feel cooler, even though the room itself hasn’t changed temperature.

Your air conditioner works differently. Rather than cooling your body, it’s removing heat and humidity from your home. A ceiling fan can help circulate that conditioned air while you’re in the room, but by itself, it won’t turn an 80-degree room into a 75-degree one. As Chambers puts it, “Your fans are not really cooling the house. You may feel cool because the fan air is moving over you, but the temperature is what it is in the house.”

What are the issues with leaving your ceiling fan on all day?

If no one is there to enjoy the breeze, leaving the fan running comes with a few downsides.

It increases your energy bill

Ceiling fans are efficient, but they’re not free to run. Most modern ceiling fans, including Energy Star models, use roughly 15 to 100 watts of electricity, depending on size and speed. That may sound small compared with an air conditioner, but it adds up over time.

If a mid-range fan runs continuously for a month, it can consume 50 to 100-plus kilowatt-hours of electricity. That’s enough to add roughly $10 to $20 per month per fan in many U.S. homes, depending on local energy rates. Run it all summer, and you’re easily looking at the cost of a couple of takeout dinners for a room no one is in.

It can actually warm the room (slightly)

It sounds counterintuitive, but every electric motor produces heat as a by-product of doing its job. A ceiling fan won’t turn your living room into a sauna, but it does gently offset some of the cooling you’re trying to maintain.

In a closed room, that small heat output—combined with constant air movement—means the fan is not actively lowering the temperature. Instead, it’s simply redistributing air that’s already there. “There’s no such thing as cool air being created by a fan … only heat being moved around or removed by air conditioning,” says Chambers.

So while you won’t feel the difference on a thermometer, the idea that the fan is “pre-cooling” an empty room doesn’t hold up.

It adds wear and tear

Like any appliance with a motor, ceiling fans have a finite lifespan measured in operating hours. Most residential ceiling fans are designed to last around 10 to 15 years under normal use, but running them continuously accelerates that clock.

Every hour of unnecessary operation adds up: more strain on bearings, more vibration over time and a greater chance of needing maintenance or replacement sooner than expected. In other words, it’s not that the fan will fail tomorrow; it’s that you’re spending its lifespan while getting no real benefit in return.

It can stir up dust

If blades aren’t cleaned regularly, they collect particles that get redistributed every time the fan spins. That’s more than a cosmetic issue. Dust in indoor air is a known irritant, and for people with allergies or asthma, it can make symptoms worse.

Chambers emphasizes that maintenance matters. “You definitely want to keep it clean,” he says. “The dirtier the fan blade gets, it uses more power to operate that fan.” In other words, dust gets blown around and makes the motor work harder, reducing efficiency.

Chambers recommends cleaning ceiling-fan blades about once a month, or more frequently in homes with pets or high dust levels. It’s a small chore, but one that keeps both air quality and efficiency in check.

What will actually cool down your room?

A ceiling fan is still one of the best tools for staying comfortable. You just have to use it correctly.

In summer, the blades should rotate counterclockwise to push air downward. Most ceiling fans have a small switch near the motor housing that lets you reverse the blade direction with the seasons. In winter, switching the fan to a clockwise rotation at a low speed helps recirculate warm air that collects near the ceiling.

To help your air conditioner work more efficiently, Chambers also recommends blocking heat before it gets inside. Blackout curtains or shades can reduce solar heat gain during the hottest part of the day, and UV-blocking window film (available at most home-improvement stores) can help keep sunny rooms from heating up as quickly.

What else should you keep in mind about your ceiling fan?

The right fan can make a bigger difference than many homeowners realize. Chambers says people often choose smaller, decorative fans based on looks, but a fan that’s too small for the room won’t circulate air effectively. Larger spaces need longer blades that can move air across the room, not just directly beneath the fixture.

If you’re tempted to leave the fan running while you’re out, you’re not alone. “Quite often we see people who just leave their fan running,” Chambers says. But the cooling they’re counting on isn’t happening. Save the breeze for when you’re actually in the room, and let your air conditioner do the job it was designed to do.

Sources:

  • Marlon Chambers, Thumbtack pro and owner of Climate Control Systems in Maryland; interviewed, June 2026
  • Energy Star: “Certified Ceiling Fans”
  • Pick HVAC: “Ceiling Fan Electricity Usage”

Why You're Always Freezing at the Airport

The Best Bed Cooling Systems We've Tested

The post Should You Leave a Ceiling Fan Running All Day? We Settle the Debate Once and for All appeared first on Reader's Digest.

articles.title: Let’s Celebrate America’s Big 2-5-0!
articles.content: 为一个拥有一切的国家在里程碑生日送什么礼物？我们当然不想空手赴宴，因此我们精心准备了一份终极《读者文摘》大礼。我们搜集了美国历史上你从未知道的精彩有趣事实，赞颂我们热爱美国的一切，并精选了造访每个州的最新鲜、最有趣、最有吸引力的理由。是的，这正如听起来一样有趣，别担心——我们不会保密。继续阅读，了解我们的派对礼盒里到底有什么，一起庆祝我们国家伟大而充满可能的一切。美国，250岁生日快乐！在《读者文摘》获取Read Up新闻通讯，获取更多有趣事实、节日、历史、幽默、清洁、旅行和科技资讯，整周精彩不断。这只是开始。点击这里发现更多精彩内容，展示每个州的精华，让你在这个伟大国家中探索畅游、大饱口福。毕竟，美国属于我们所有人，你也值得被庆祝。再次祝美国250岁生日快乐——愿再续辉煌250年！《读者文摘》致力于由具有专业领域知识和经验的 writers 创作高质量内容，并咨询相关合格专家。我们依靠政府、专业组织和学术机构等权威原始来源，以及作者在适当情况下的个人经历。我们验证所有事实和数据，以可靠来源支撑，并随着时间推移持续回顾，确保其保持准确和最新。了解我们的团队、撰稿人和编辑政策的更多信息。这篇帖子《让我们庆祝美国的大日子2-5-0！》首次发表于《读者文摘》。
articles.summary:
What do you get the country that has everything for its milestone birthday? We certainly didn’t want to show up to this party empty-handed, so we decided on the ultimate Reader’s Digest gift. We’ve curated fascinating and quirky facts about U.S. history that you never knew, odes to all the things we love about America and the most delicious, most interesting, most fun reasons to visit each and every state.

Yes, it’s as fun as it sounds, and don’t worry—we won’t keep it a surprise. Read on to find out exactly what’s in our party package, and celebrate all the wonderful things our nation is and can be. Happy 250th, America!

And that’s just the beginning. Click here to find even more great content featuring the best things in every state, so you can explore and eat your way through this great country of ours. After all, America is about all of us, and you deserve to be celebrated too.

Again, Happy 250th, America—and here’s to another 250!

Why trust us

At Reader’s Digest, we’re committed to producing high-quality content by writers with expertise and experience in their field in consultation with relevant, qualified experts. We rely on reputable primary sources, including government and professional organizations and academic institutions as well as our writers’ personal experiences where appropriate.
